author: @andrewharpin6749
text:
1. No Polywell has produced net energy gain (at least publicly), I searched for this and found nothing, if it had happened it would be world wide news.

2. I suggest seeing this person's channel: https://www.youtube.com/@PolywellFan4512, he's a fusion researcher. Knows way more than I do about Fusion and is no longer convinced that Polywell will be the solution.

3. Is Fission dead? I doubt it, the main issue that has faced fission (other than typical fear), is lack of investment. MSRs were proposed by the person who basically invented the BWR and subsequent PWR reactors. He was supposedly shutdown as his proposal at the time was for a Fast spectrum MSR. The reason is a bit conspiracy theorist, but the logic is that this was not suitable for weapons material production and so funding was not provided.

MSRs have their issues and Fast reactors have their issues, but in the UK at least, should you be able to build a safe reliable Fast MSR, we have enough "spent" fuel in the UK to produce all our energy needs for IRO 30,000 years (at current usage). As a Fast reactor can still burn it and it will use the "Depleted Uranium" as fuel.

The issue as I mentioned above is investment, the advantage would be a significant reduction in the lifetime of a large portion of the "nuclear waste". 


The reason I mention this is that even if Fusion was solved tomorrow, and we could immediately start building power plants for that solution, it would take time to create these generating stations and the current waste issues from Fission do not disappear overnight.

author: @JA-ti3wd
text: 1:09 Japan restarted 2 reactors in 2015 and there are 9 running now. A further 16 are undergoing start up approvals.  4:15 they have achieved fusion through tokmak reactors and even energy positive reactions (I.e. more energy out than in). What they haven't done yet is made a commercial viable machine. That's what ITER seeks to do. 10:48 We're still building and designing fission stations. There's even talk about trialling an SMR station in the UK so I don't think they're going away anytime soon.

author: @Ken00001010
text: All should keep in mind that there are three important phases to the development of fusion power. The first is to be able to do fusion at all. These fusers do that, and so do other techniques. The second phase is to be able to yield more energy out than has to be put in. Some experimental fusion reactors may be within a decade of going energy positive. The third phase (this is the very hard one) is to be able to build fusion power plants that over their service life achieve a levelized cost of energy (LCOE) that is lower than other ways of getting that energy at the projected future time point. So if there were a successful fusion design achieved in the next 10 years, it would still take at least another 10 years to develop and test a commercial power plant. Then that would have to compete on cost with renewables (that have no fuel cost at all) which are expected to continue to drop in LCOE.

author: @jjamespacbell
text: Nuclear fission is a worthy scientific research but, after 70+ years of so many great minds and massive amounts of money with no net positive results, it may be time to face the fact that base electricity will be created in the near future with solar/wind/storage (chemical/hydro/etc). The good news is that the economics and not environmental data will drive that decision. At any cost, we need to stop burning stuff.

author: @atrumluminarium
text:
There are more types of nuclear power, that rather than rely on the strong force directly like fission and fusion, rely on the decay of radioactive isotopes. Namely RTGs, radioisotope stirling, betavoltaic and alphavoltaic. 

Betavoltaic cells can actually be "hacked" together from household materials by using a small PV cell and wrapping it around a couple of tritium gun-sights. Basically you get a battery that outputs fairly low power but can keep providing power for about 12 years (1 half life).

While the power output is fairly limited (especially compared to fission),  a way to squeeze out a little extra power from the residual radioactivity in nuclear waste rather than just leaving it to dissipate unused. For example RTGs were used in the USSR to power lights in remote lighthouses and radioisotope stirling was also considered by NASA at one point for the longer space missions.

Regarding the "downsides" of fission, I believe that with SMRs and graphite coated pellets these worries might be a thing of the past once they are commercially available. It is a fact that most of the reactors we have are old and hence not up to date with the latest advancement in nuclear engineering simply due to fear and misinformed climate activists.

replies.author: @travismoore7849
replies.text: Nuclear fusion was made using liquid nitrogen to make a pyroelectric field to strip electrons off hydrogen to get atoms close enough to fuse. Now what if you made a tube with hydrogen in it. And at the center suspended by insulators you use a capacitor made of graphite and barium titanate that is charged by a magnetron to make a high voltage electric field to strip electrons off of hydrogen. And a coil could be made to resonate with that capacitor by induction to make a ion resonator that strips electrons off hydrogen for making fusion.

Now imagine you want it to contain a small piece of the Sun in the middle.
Remember how large the Sun is, and how large the solar system is and how much gravity AND electro-magnetic fields hold it where it is.
Now remember how many nuclear 'incidents' there have been....

author: @russellzauner
text:
I live near Trojan (just a few miles away).  I'm gonna go ahead and say I'd rather not see more nuclear plants - we don't need them, if everyone is harvesting/generating where they are at then the utilities should put in two way meters by default and we'd have a giant cooperative, and places where the utilities are cooperative the prices are low and the reliability and supply high, at least in the USA.  We have mostly coops in Oregon and our energy costs are not the lowest but overall people that move here keep being surprised that the grid is cheap and stable here.  If everyone was feeding in small bits as part of their new building energy certification then eventually the BIG power generations centers will start to decommission from attrition over time, users being preferential to all the advantages of going local none of the disadvantages of being dependent on remote power, especially in times of weather upheaval, et al.

It's all nice but the simpler and less complicated and more distributed we can make the systems the more consumers will be of the mind that they'd rather use the power that's "Just There" instead of waiting, which only leads to broader adoption, integration, mainstreaming, and ultimately, normalization, at which point we'll have the kids being born then asking the question not "why we don't" but "why did we ever".

author: @CCoburn3
text:
Nuclear fission has a bad reputation because the Luddites have generated so much untrue propaganda that the people have bought into their lies. Nuclear fission power is safe and economical. We have enough thorium to provide all the electricity the world will need for at least the next 500 years. That includes generating enough electricity to bring everyone in the world up to today's standard in the US and Western Europe and maintaining that power level with the increasing population.

As for nuclear fusion, the "30 years in the future" meme is overly optimistic. It is based on the ready availability of deuterium and tritium -- neither of which is available in quantities that are necessary for fusion power. All that being said, the Pollywell (or a variation thereof) is probably the most likely version to work. But even the Pollywell system is not going to have the feedstocks necessary to provide nuclear power for the world. (But fusion research WILL continue to generate TREMENDOUS amounts of money for physicists. So, as long as governments can continue to pump tax money into that bottomless pit, the research will continue.)

author: @urporavi8579

replies.author: @NathanMyers-c8y
replies.text: Not true. It released orders of magnitude _less_ energy than was used. What it released more of was energy they succeeded in actually delivering to the target. And, there is no path to practical generation from that technology: it is useful only for simulating nuclear weapons, which is where they get their funding. All the participants have top nuke security clearances, for that reason.

author: @TheBaconWizard
text: I am watching with great interest the development of pulsed fusion reactors of various kinds: Helion in the USA and First Light in the UK. Basically, they aren't interested in attempting the covetted self-sustaining reaction that eludes us so-far, but simply getting more out than they put in... and so they pulse it, collect the energy, clear and rechage the chamber and run it again. I am guessing they use the Polywell system.

replies.author: @NathanMyers-c8y
replies.text: The only fusion startup that has any possibility of a profit is the one that builds prototypes for dodgy fusion startups to use scamming their investors. There will never be so much as a single erg of commercial energy produced via fusion, because extracting useful energy would cost much more than for fission, and fission is already very far from competitive with solar and wind.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: @NathanMyers-c8y The means of extracting power is the same as containing the plasma, just as an electric motor is the same as a generator. It's already built-in. Whether it will reach commercially viable speeds I couldn't say.

replies.author: @garry8390
replies.text: Fision is cheap, Fusion is nonsense, grow up.

replies.author: @NathanMyers-c8y
replies.text:
@TheBaconWizard You refer to Helion's project, the only one working on D-3He fusion. But at the temperature they propose to work at, deuterium is orders of magnitude more eager to fuse with other deuterium than with (3He) helions. And, 3He is scarce. No, we will not be able to mine it on the moon.

All of the other fusion startups are promoting D-D fusion, which emits hot neutrons, which need to be absorbed for their heat.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: @NathanMyers-c8y Yeah, First Light plan to use heat exchange between the lithium and water for steam.
